question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will adopt the road network within Essex Ridge Laurelvale, Tandragee.
answer_text: Road adoption by the Department is governed by The Private Streets (NI) Order 1980 (The Order) and roads, including those within private developments such as Essex Ridge, will only be considered for adoption if brought to standard through the Private Streets Determination process or by owners/frontages in accordance with Article 9 of the Order.Unfortunately Essex Ridge is a private development which does not have a road bond in place and the Department would therefore not be in a position to contribute towards the works required to bring the road up to its standards. In this instance all costs would be the responsibility of the residents and property owners.On the 25th September I announced the introduction of new Private Streets Enforcement Guidance for staff.  This guidance will greatly reduce the risk of developments, constructed from January 2024, remaining unadopted.  My officials continue to monitor the progress of all housing developments and engage with developers to ensure timely adoption through the normal Private Streets process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how she plans to incentivise employers to offer more apprenticeship opportunities.
answer_text: Participation on my Department’s Apprenticeship programmes is at a record level with over fourteen thousand apprentices presently enrolled, with particularly strong growth in Higher Level Apprenticeships, offering employers access to the skills they need.My Department funds the directed, off-the-job training component of apprenticeships, with annual funding of £48 million.  Businesses are eligible for an employer incentive payment upon the apprentice’s successful achievement of a Level 2 or Level 3 apprenticeship framework, ranging from £670 to £2,008 depending on the occupational area.  This is a 20% 5 compared to the previous programme. In addition, The Construction Industry Training Board offers a series of Employer Apprentice Grants to further support construction sector employers.Finally, my Department’s Apprenticeship Action Plan includes measures such as the introduction of apprenticeship navigators, which will provide local support to employers seeking to employ an apprentice, and the apprenticeship inclusion challenge fund, supporting employers to work collaboratively with educators and the community and voluntary sector to encourage wider participation.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any engagement they have had with the Chancellor of the Exchequer in relation to the Autumn Budget 2025.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any further action that is planned to support an increase in the availability of apprenticeship opportunities.
answer_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, to outline any further action that is planned to support an increase in the availability of apprenticeship opportunitiesApprenticeships are employment opportunities determined by employer needs and developing our apprenticeship system is a key priority. Collaborative efforts, including sectoral, regional, and cross-border partnerships, are creating clearer pathways and supporting the growth of the apprenticeship system with 200 pathways available and investment by the Department of over £48m annually.Participation is at a record level with over fourteen thousand apprentices presently enrolled in programmes offered by my Department, reflecting an increase in both employer and participant engagement. While apprenticeships have grown in popularity and success, challenges remain for young people seeking employment in specific sectors.FE Colleges are integral to preparing young people for vocational pathways, providing the skills, knowledge, and experience required for progression, especially in priority skills areas. Colleges work with councils, sector bodies, and regional initiatives to align support with local economic needs and learner priorities.Annual initiatives such as Apprenticeship Week and the Apprenticeship Awards promote the value and visibility of apprenticeships, encouraging wider participation. The Apprenticeship Action Plan aims to increase employer engagement, with measures like piloting Apprenticeship Navigators to assist SMEs recruiting their first apprentice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the speed limit on the Corcreevy Road, Richhill, could be extended to cover the access point to Richhill Presbyterian church.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. When considering the appropriate speed limit for a road, the Department’s policy is used to ensure speed limits are consistent and motorists are presented with the same speed limit on similar roads. This takes into account factors such as the characteristic of the road, the speed of existing traffic, the degree of frontage development, and road traffic collision data, which is continuously monitored using data provided by the PSNI.Officials will undertake a review of the existing speed limit on this stretch of Corcreevy Road to ensure they are in line with our policy document and considered to be appropriate along with the existing warning signs and road markings at this location.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the next steps regarding the Civil Service vacating Marlborough House.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when Derryhale Primary School, Craigavon, will be included in the roll-out of part-time 20mph speed limit zones.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer for everyone. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools across the North and are listed on my Department’s website, link; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/20mph-speed-limit-schools-tranche-3.This will bring the total delivered across the North since 2020/21 to 273 schools.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.While the Tranche 3 programme focuses on schools on rural roads where the national speed limit applies, I am aware that there are schools in urban and residential areas which would also benefit from a reduction in speed limits. I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking and cycling.My Department is also carrying out a review on a number of speed related matters including 20mph speed limits in general and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise as part of this review.  Further consideration on the use of part time speed limits at schools will be taken on completion of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a footpath resurfacing scheme can be taken forward for Ballybeg Park, Tandragee, given the defects and problems associated with the current flagstone surface.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many locations that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, footway resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the disabled parking bay, located on Main Street, Richhill, will be refreshed.
answer_text: Officials have issued a request for the refreshing of this disabled parking bay to the Department’s road marking contractor.  The work, subject to favourable weather conditions, is expected for completion in the next few weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reason for the delay in the application of loading bay road markings, serving Main Street, Markethill.
answer_text: I apologise for the delay to the installation of the loading bay markings, my officials had issued a work instruction for its completion but unfortunately due to other high priority commitments, the contractor was unable to undertake this work.A new road marking contract has recently been awarded and a revised works order has been issued under this contract. It is hoped that the new road markings will be completed in the next few weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the introduction of planning application validation checklists, on application processing times.
answer_text: The legislation to give local planning authorities power to introduce planning application validation checklists came into operation on 1st April 2025. It is too early to conduct any meaningful analysis to consider the impact that the introduction of the validation checklist process has had on application processing times. Going forward, my Department will monitor where possible, the impact of the introduction of validation checklists on performance across the system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any current initiatives directly targeted towards the improvement of wellbeing.
answer_text: My Department delivers, or funds ALBs to deliver, a range of initiatives directly targeted towards the improvement of wellbeing:The Neighbourhood Renewal programme provides sustained support to areas of deprivation across Northern Ireland has seen an investment of approximately £22m in 2025/26.Make the Call Wraparound Service ensures that all individuals and their families are receiving the benefits, support and services they are entitled to claim.Historic Environment Division completed a pilot programme in March 2025 addressing the role State Care Monuments have in contributing to key objectives on wellbeing and social inclusion.National Museums NI delivers a range of programmes and activities, supported by funding from my Department, by providing access to its collection and utilising partnership opportunities.The ‘Move More, Live Better’ campaign, a digital campaign aimed at encouraging participation in sport and physical activity was launched in August 2025. Through the Arts Council, my Department provides funding for the Arts and Mental Health Partnership Programme and the Arts and Older People Programme which aims to give creative opportunity for people living with mental health issues The Armagh Observatory & Planetarium delivers community-focused programming that enhances the quality of life for individuals and communities.Libraries NI delivers a wide range of programmes, services and facilities which make a significant contribution the improvement of wellbeing.Welfare support and benefits contribute to the improvement of wellbeing. This includes the provision of welfare mitigations, Discretionary Support, and employability interventions.As part of the Tenancy Support & Sustainment Strategy 2025-2030 the NIHE has implemented a number of services and projects to support the wellbeing of their tenants. They have provided funding to voluntary, community and social enterprise organisations to deliver projects that support the sustainability and resilience of Housing Executive tenancies.  Through the Financial Inclusion Strategy 2023-28 and Making Your Money Work service, the NIHE offers various pathways to empower tenants in improving their finances, lessening poverty and mitigating the overall impact financial struggles can have on their wellbeing.The Homeless Prevention fund programme for 2025/26 supports a range of projects which provide financial advice and, in some cases, direct financial assistance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Connect2 hub for Newry.
answer_text: Six Connect 2 Hubs were opened in August 2022: at Rathgael House (Bangor), Ballykelly (Jubilee House), Downpatrick (Rathkeltair House), Ballymena (County Hall), Craigavon (Marlborough House), and Omagh (County Hall). Latest data, from August 2024 to July 2025, shows an average usage over the six hubs of 20%.Given the lower than expected usage of the Hubs, plans for the remaining Hubs have been put on hold due to current financial constraints. The Hybrid Working policy has recently been reviewed to include use of Connect2 hubs as the workplace on an exceptional basis and any impact on usage levels will be kept under review.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the importance of engaging with leaders from other jurisdictions and administrations in order to promote Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on progress regarding the bridge repairs under reference BR41580 at Annareagh Road, Richhill.
answer_text: Repair works to Bridge BR41580 at Annareagh Road, Richhill were initially scheduled for delivery in the 2024/25 financial year.  As you will be aware, my department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years, and unfortunately due to funding constraints and the need to prioritise other structures presenting more urgent safety concerns, this work could not be completed.A detailed inspection of the bridge is scheduled for later this year. As part of this process, vegetation will be cleared from the parapet walls to allow for a thorough assessment. Minor repairs identified during this inspection, as well as any outstanding issues noted in previous inspections, will also be completed as part of this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a timeline for repairs to the historic bridge in Clare village, Tandragee.
answer_text: The bridge in Clare sustained significant damage when a large adjacent mature tree was blown over by the high winds of Storm Eowyn in January 2025.  My Department assessed the damage at the time and have since issued instruction to a contractor to take forward repairs.  Works are programmed to commence at the start of July for a period of approximately 3 weeks, however this is dependent on favourable weather conditions and the timely completion of adjacent works by NI Water contractors.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether work to redesign the Northern Ireland Assembly website is still due to commence in July 2025.
answer_text: The procurement of a digital agency to support the Assembly Commission in the redevelopment of its website and provision of a new Content Management System is in progress, using a Crown Commercial Services Digital Outcomes framework agreement. This competition is a two-stage process.The stage 1 invitation to framework suppliers was published in April 2025 and suppliers were shortlisted to proceed to stage 2 at the end of May 2025. Stage 2 closed on 20 June 2025. Assessment by the evaluation panel will take place during July. Once procurement procedures have been completed, an award of contract is expected in late August 2025 with work commencing in early September 2025. Parallel enabling project work by the Assembly Commission’s IS Office has been continuing to modernise the Assembly’s AIMS platform, which will make a ‘step change’ in the way IT applications and services are delivered. This is closely linked to enabling many Assembly business-related functions of the new website.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on the recent meeting of the Council of the Nations and Regions.
answer_text: We attended the second meeting of the Council of the Nations and Regions in London on Friday 23 May.The meeting was chaired by the Prime Minister.  Also attending were the Deputy Prime Minister, the Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster, the First Ministers of Scotland and Wales, the Mayor of London, and the Mayors of Combined Authorities and County Combined Authorities in England.  The meeting provided an opportunity to discuss recent trade agreements with the EU, US and India, and also opportunities around Growth and AI. There was considerable agreement on future priorities, including more investment in infrastructure, and the need to ensure our young people learn about AI, to provide them with the knowledge and skills which they will need in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the process for the (i) assessment; and (ii) installation of traffic calming measures.
answer_text: Traffic calming requests are assessed according to Policy & Procedure Guide RSPPG E027, which can be found at the following link Road safety engineering procedures RSPPG E027 | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)Traffic calming is funded from within the Local Transport and Safety Measures budget allocated to each Division and installations of such measures are taken forward by the respective division through the relevant Minor Works Term Contractor.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any further action he will take to improve the timeframes associated with the Northern Ireland Environment Agency responding to planning application consultations.
answer_text: As a statutory consultee within the planning system, DAERA’s performance is measured against a duty to respond to a statutory consultation within 21 calendar days or otherwise agreed timescale.I am aware of the frustrations caused by the time it takes my department as a statutory consultee to respond to some planning consultations. Consequently, my officials are developing a Planning Improvement Plan in 2025/26. This plan will include a range of measures to improve planning performance and service and will covers 5 strands: Technical advice and guidance.Caseload managementMonitoring and reporting on performance.Process and IT systems.  Communication and stakeholder engagement.Current measures already being undertaken in addition to the Planning Improvement Plan, include:a 3-month Prioritisation trial which commenced, in collaboration with Planning Authorities, on 3 March 2025.  Operationally, the overarching principle is that that this applies for exceptional reasons only. a review of staff resourcing and vacancy management as I recognise that this is one of the key areas in reducing the ongoing caseload levels. My department is therefore actively seeking to recruit additional staff for this work area.a review and update of internal processes, procedures and guidance including Standing Advice available on the DAERA website.analysis of consultations being receipted to inform targeted measures to reduce the level of reconsultation and improve the quality of information being submitted with a consultation. One development type being targeted through this is single dwelling consultations.engagement with other jurisdictions.In addition, my department is working in collaboration with the DfI cross-governmental Planning Statutory Consultee Forum and has contributed to a number of exercises aiming to drive forward continuous improvements in the planning system.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of (i) the UK-India trade deal; and (ii) the impact of the ‘double contribution convention’ for National Insurance contributions which is included in the deal.
answer_text: The British Government has not yet published an impact assessment for its trade deal with India, however its recently published ‘preliminary estimate’ is for a +0.1% change to the British economy by 2040. The same preliminary estimate suggests that British exports to India will grow by £15.7 billion by 2040, whilst Indian exports to Britain will grow by £9.8 billion. These estimates include the North of Ireland but are not disaggregated as such. The north does not have a significant bilaterial trading relationship with India, by value or volume. The most recent data shows that 143 businesses across the north (from a VAT and/or PAYE registered business base of around 80k) exported £65m of goods (2024) and £176m of services (2022) to India.  It is hoped the reduction in tariffs, easier access for service providers and streamlining of custom procedures associated with the trade deal will help current and new exporters from the North to access the Indian market. I have not made any assessment of the ‘double contribution convention as this relates to social security which is outside my remit.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide a summary of their engagement at the most recent meeting of the Council of the Nations and Regions.
answer_text: We attended the second meeting of the Council of the Nations and Regions in London on Friday 23 May.  The meeting was chaired by the Prime Minister.  Also attending were the Deputy Prime Minister, the Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster, the First Ministers of Scotland and Wales, the Mayor of London, and the Mayors of Combined Authorities and County Combined Authorities in England.  The meeting provided us with an opportunity to engage with the PM on a number of important issues for Northern Ireland, recent trade agreements, and also opportunities around the use Artificial Intelligence technologies to support Growth.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a fresh assessment can be made to enable the installation of a 40mph speed limit at Milltown on the Aghory Road, Portadown.
answer_text: My Department’s policy for setting the appropriate speed limit for a road aims to ensure speed limits are consistent and motorists are presented with the same speed limit on similar roads taking into account factors such as: the characteristic of the road; the speed of existing traffic; the degree of frontage development; and road traffic collision data, which is continuously monitored using data provided by the PSNI. A review of injury collision data from the PSNI shows that there was one recorded injury collision along this particular stretch of the Aghory Road within the last 3 recorded years.  The current speed limits on the Aghory Road are in line with our policy document and considered to be appropriate.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding, or parking which causes an obstruction to other road (and footway) users should be directed to the police using the non-emergency number 101.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of the levels of bovine TB in Northern Ireland, to outline his plans to address tuberculosis in wildlife.
answer_text: Rates of bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) in Northern Ireland remain much too high.  I have been clear since becoming Minister, that something must change, and this change must be based on science-led and evidence-based solutions capable of delivering real and meaningful reductions in bTB levels. This needs to happen urgently and can only be progressed with both stakeholders and government working collaboratively together. That is why I established the TB Partnership Steering Group earlier this year, a new body consisting of key stakeholders across farming, industry, veterinary and environmental organisations.  This Group has worked with my officials since January to develop a new delivery plan and I was pleased to endorse the Group’s agreed Bovine TB in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication which was published on the 15th of April.The Blueprint is founded on the principle that eradicating bTB requires a unified, multisectoral approach.  It sets out work needed in almost 40 actions across three key themes- People, Cattle and Wildlife.  It builds upon the Chief Veterinary Officer (CVO)’s review of bTB in Northern Ireland, which was published in November 2024, which itself took account of the actions within the 2022 bTB Eradication Strategy. Internally, a new Bovine Tuberculosis Eradication Transformation Programme has also been established to deliver the actions set out in the Blueprint, some of which will need further public consultation; new or amending legislation; and additional resources before implementation. With respect to wildlife specifically, my officials continue to work on the issues and gaps identified by the judicial review ruling in October 2023, which quashed my predecessor’s decision to advance a non-selective cull of badgers in areas of high TB incidence.  This work will provide me with detailed advice on potential wildlife intervention options and involves looking at the latest scientific evidence; the potential legislative options relating to an intervention; and updating figures relating to how much these interventions could cost.  I anticipate having this advice before summer.  A full public consultation will also be needed before I can make a fully informed, science-led decision on wildlife intervention.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the measures that will be used to determine the location of the 600 houses proposed to be purchased by the Housing Executive for temporary accommodation over the next three years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The proposed acquisition of 600 properties by NIHE over a 3-year period will be focused on reducing NIHE’s current exposure to high-cost temporary accommodation (particularly hotels and B&Bs).Accordingly, the principal considerations will include:Geographical areas where exposure to high-cost temporary accommodation is most prevalent.The nature of the temporary accommodation client groups (single people, couples, families).The availability of suitable properties to purchase that align with the above criteria.Value for money considerations.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.The planned acquisition of 600 properties will be focused on reducing NIHE’s current exposure to high-cost temporary accommodation (particularly hotels and B&Bs).While the final locations have yet to be determined the principal considerations will include:Geographical areas where exposure to high-cost temporary accommodation is most prevalent.The nature of the temporary accommodation client groups (single people, couples, families).The availability of suitable properties to purchase that align with the above criteria.Value for money considerations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the energy efficiency measures that will be retrofitted to the 600 houses proposed to be purchased by the Housing Executive for temporary accommodation over the next three years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“It is not possible to confirm what works (including energy efficiency works) will be required in relation to the proposal to purchase an additional 600 properties to address current temporary accommodation pressures.The identification and purchase of individual properties will be driven by a number of factors including:Geographical areas where exposure to high-cost temporary accommodation is most prevalent.The nature of the temporary accommodation client groups (single persons, couples, families).The availability of suitable property available to purchase that align with the above criteria.Additionally, securing Value for Money in the purchase of individual properties will be a key consideration. This will include an assessment of the market value and extent of required works on a property-by-property basis prior to property purchase.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the pothole defects within the 30mph speed limits in Richhill village, County Armagh, will be repaired.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. As a result of these budget constraints, my Department’s road maintenance standards have been reduced to a Limited Service, so that only the highest priority defects across the network are being repaired. I have been advised that the roads in Richhill village are due their next inspection in the coming days and any defects meeting my Department’s Limited Service will be instructed for repair. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any plans he has to improve access for patients of NHS dental services in Newry and Armagh.
To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will consider adding disused farm dwellings to the exclusion list for rating of empty homes.
answer_text: Rating of empty property is intended to encourage all owners of empty property to make decisions on use whether as a home or otherwise. It is something that all owners of empty homes face, but regardless of whether they are empty or not farmhouses already get 20% reduction in the capital value. Likewise, farmers pay no rates at present on agricultural buildings. I am therefore not considering adding disused farm dwellings to the exclusion list for rating of empty homes.Where a property is considered no longer usable, a review of the valuation can be carried out, and if a property is considered truly derelict then it will be removed from the valuation list. Any additional rate relief means either foregoing of revenue or charging other ratepayers more. Exemptions or relief is often viewed from the perspective of “who benefits”, but there is a real cost. Every pound raised through the rating system stays here as a resource to help pay for hospitals, schools, and other essential regional services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his plans to repair the damage caused by Storm Éowyn to Gosford Forest Park.
answer_text: Storm Éowyn has caused catastrophic damage to Gosford Forest Park both to the trees and the recreational infrastructure.  Forest Service and its recreation partner Armagh City Banbridge & Craigavon Borough Council, have over the past 4 weeks, restored public access to the park and cleared all trees from the internal forest tracks to allow pedestrian access.The more major harvesting interventions will commence in the next few weeks and will be ongoing for several months.  Harvesting of these heavily impacted areas is the necessary first intervention that will allow subsequent re-instatement of the extensive trail network, including off-road cycling.This will also enable the re-establishment of the cleared areas in line with longer term objectives and provide opportunities to expediate plans to increase diversity of species and forest resilience throughout the wider Gosford Forest area.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the value of vehicle damage claims paid out to drivers for damage sustained on roads in the Newry and Armagh constituency in 2024.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to roads related public liability claims can be found online at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20192020 Please note that my Department maintains its records by Roads Section Office, which broadly align with Council areas, and not by Assembly constituency areas.  In addition, my Department maintains its records by financial year rather than by calendar year.  Table 7 in the above link provides details of the vehicle damage compensation paid out during the last five full financial years broken down by Roads Section Office. This includes the figures for the full financial year 2023/24 for the Armagh, Banbridge & Craigavon and Newry, Mourne & Down section offices, which cover the Newry and Armagh constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the proposed actions that the Task and Finish Group will undertake as part of his Department’s zero tolerance approach to benefit fraud.
To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 21042/22-27, to provide the requested information.
answer_text: My Department provides funding to support managing authorities and sectoral bodies across education, each with autonomy to tailor their support to their sector. The Independent Review of Education reflected on school management and support arrangements, highlighting the sub-optimal arrangements for controlled schools in particular. I have recently announced changes to address this matter, establishing dedicated structures in the interests of greater equity and consistency across our main education sectors. I want to see success across all of our school sectors, and I will always be open to further system changes where needed in future, including school management and support services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what support his Department will provide in the aftermath of Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: In the immediate aftermath of the Storm, I activated and extended the scope of the Non-flooding Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance to provide financial assistance to councils. I ring-fenced funding of up to £1m for outreach services, delivered in partnership with councils and the community and voluntary sector, to ensure the right supports were in place in the right way for those most in need. The scheme provided funding for community assistance centres, emergency supplies of hot meals, transport to and from centres and other services, and washing facilities. The member will be aware that there has been a call for compensation payments to those affected by the loss of electricity, this would not be for my Department to provide and will be a decision for the Executive.     In the meantime, my Department’s Finance Support service is available for people who find themselves in a financial crisis, including those experiencing financial difficulty as a result of a disaster, e.g. fire, flooding, etc. The service provides financial help and support through the provision of Discretionary Support, Social Fund and Universal Credit Contingency Fund. Through local Councils my Department also supports Social Supermarket projects in each area. These projects provide access to food alongside wraparound services to address the underlying issues contributing to household’s financial position.	In addition, householders can contact the Department’s “Make the Call Service” to ensure they are getting all the money and supports they are entitled to.  Make The Call offers a telephony service and has a team of Community Outreach Officers who work from regional locations across Northern Ireland to conduct home visits and provide face to face assistance and support with benefit claim completion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the time taken for responding to planning application consultations by his Department within the Newry and Armagh constituency.
answer_text: As Newry and Armagh are in 2 separate council areas I can advise as follows:Newry, Mourne and Down District CouncilTotal number of consultations receipted (Statutory & Non-Statutory) from 01 April 24 to 23 January 25 is 329;Total number of consultations responded to on time is 178 (54%);Total number of consultations responded to late is 98 (30%);Total number of consultation responses still within target is 22 (7%);Total number of consultation responses outstanding is 31 (9%). Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough CouncilTotal number of consultations receipted (Statutory & Non-Statutory) from 01 April 24 to 23 January 25 is 591;Total number of consultations responded to on time is 371 (63%);Total number of consultations responded to late is 158 (27%);Total number of consultation responses still within target is 17 (3%);Total number of consultation responses outstanding is 45 (7%).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the time taken for responding to planning application consultations by his Department within the Newry and Armagh constituency.
answer_text: My Department does not collate or manage information on a constituency basis, instead it is collated by local council area.  Newry and Armagh constituency includes areas of both Newry, Mourne and Down Council (NM&DC) and Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Council (ABCC) areas.I can advise that the Southern area of my department receives a higher number of applications than other division, and that 80% of the 3,600 statutory consultations received during the 2023/24 year were responded to within the 21 day target, with a similar percentage attained in the 2022/23 year.  My Department has worked hard to maintain and improve the performance of the statutory consultation process over the last number of years and continues to do so within the limited resources available.  Based on current statistics for 2024/25, response rates are in line with the performance levels of previous years. My Department’s officials continue to work with councils in the areas listed to continue to  improve the overall service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the total number of acres of agricultural land vested; and (ii) the total monies paid out for agricultural land, to enable the construction of the carriageway from Dungannon to Ballygawley.
answer_text: I can advise that the total amount of land vested for construction of the carriageway from Dungannon to Ballygawley was 166 hectares (410 acres). A breakdown of the agricultural land vested is not held by my Department.    The total figure paid to landowners was £27,432,511. This will include agricultural, commercial and residential categories. A breakdown of the monies paid directly for agricultural land is not held by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the agricultural land valuations, per acre, used at the time to quantify the costs of construction of the dual carriageway from Dungannon to Ballygawley, completed in 2010.
answer_text: My Department does not retain details on the agricultural land valuations, per acre, used for the construction of the A4 in 2010.Land and Property Services would have provided advice to my Department on the assessment of compensation following the exercise of compulsory purchase powers on this project.  The Department for Finance may be best placed to provide this further detail.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the impact of excessive alcohol consumption on visits to accident and emergency facilities over the Christmas and New Year period.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how the Northern Ireland Environment Agency is progressing with the backlog of planning applications.
answer_text: Last year the Northern Ireland Environment Agency responded to all agricultural planning applications that were within the backlog due to previous pauses in the provision of Ammonia emissions advice during 2023.Although the measures put in place to address these backlog cases were welcomed, the business-as-usual backlog of planning applications remains a problem for the NIEA. While many of the longest outstanding cases were responded to and the average time to respond to consultations was maintained in 2024, there was not a reduction in the overall number of backlog cases during this time period.The improvement of the NIEAs contribution to the planning system is a key priority for me, and I have asked for measures to be put in place which, once established, will help to reduce backlogs and response timeframes. A key measure in these regards is seeking to recruit additional permanent staff members for this important work area in 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 18854/22-27, whether he will reassess the decision to remove lighting at the location following a night-time collision between a car and a Translink bus on the evening of 10 December 2024.
answer_text: Road safety remains a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users. As such, my officials reviewed data provided by the PSNI with regard to this road traffic collision which confirmed the removal of street lighting was not a contributing factor to the incident.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will implement a 20mph speed limit at (i) Derryhale Primary School, Portadown; and (ii) Clare Primary School, Craigavon.
answer_text: A review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken, shared with the Committee for Infrastructure and is publicly available on my Department’s internet site Review of Part-Time 20mph Speed Limits at Schools | Department for Infrastructure.The review concludes that the initiative has been widely welcomed and supported by the school community including parents and school children as well as making young people feel safer outside school grounds. The data collected as part of the review also shows that there has been a general decrease in traffic speeds at both school start and finish times as a result of the installation of the part-time speed limits. I am now considering the introduction of a further scheme to enable installations at other school locations, and more widely on the introduction of permanent 20mph speed limits in the vicinity of schools and residential areas.As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why street lighting has been removed from a section of the Markethill Road, Portadown, close to the junction of Aghory Road, given the series of road traffic accidents that have occurred in the vicinity.
answer_text: In considering those lighting installations which are deemed to have reached the end of their serviceable life and can no longer be maintained, a survey is carried out of the location and lighting is replaced only in those adopted areas which meet my Department’s policy for the provision of lighting. This can result in the removal and non-replacement of lights in areas such as back pathways, unadopted roads and footways, or rural areas with insufficient density of housing. These lights are removed for safety reasons as they can no longer be maintained, there are no instances where lights have been permanently turned off.This policy helps ensure that the limited resources available for the provision of street lighting are directed at those areas where they can provide the maximum benefit to the public, particularly where road or infrastructure safety is concerned.In line with this policy, an assessment of the area in question was undertaken and found that the density of existing development at this location fell short of the stipulated criteria which requires the presence of at least 10 dwellings within a 200m contiguous road length.  A review of PSNI statistics has not indicated any instances of night-time collisions involving injury at this location.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the availability of NHS dental treatment in the Newry and Armagh constituency.
answer_text: Access to Health Service dentistry is currently very difficult, especially for unregistered patients, and this is likely to be the case for some time. General Dental Practitioners are independent contractors and, unlike General Medical Practitioners, are under no obligation to register new Health Service patients and are able to decide which patients they will treat under Health Service arrangements. They may also de-register their existing Health Service patients with three months’ notice. Those who wish to register with a dental practice as a Health Service patient are advised to contact practices in your area directly. They may have to check with many dental practices and / or be prepared to travel outside of their local area, in order to find a practice that is currently registering Health Service patients. Further advice, including the HSC Service Finder, can be found at: Registering as a Health Service dental patient - DOH/HSCNI Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG)As part of a £9.2m financial investment for 2024/2025, a Northern Ireland-wide Dental Access Scheme (DAS) began on 1 August 2024, aimed at improving access (during the working week) for unregistered patients experiencing an emergency, urgent, or pressing dental condition.  There are 30 practices participating in the scheme, seven of which are located in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust area. Currently, DAS is providing access to dental care for around 450 patients each week.Patients can access the scheme by contacting participating practices directly. Patients will be required to outline the dental problem they are experiencing, and ask if the practice has any appointments available under the scheme. Contact details of participating practices are available on the SPPG website at: Dental Access Scheme - DOH/HSCNI Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG)In addition, undergraduate dental students in Belfast Dental Hospital provide emergency dental appointments for unregistered patients under the supervision of dental teaching staff. Patients can self-refer to this service but it is only available during university term time. Information on how to access this service can be found at:  Dental services | Belfast Health & Social Care Trust website (hscni.net)

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps he is taking to promote a career in trades among young people.
answer_text: Question 9 – AQO 1221/22-27Skilled trades are a vital part of our economy and offer fulfilling and well-paid careers.The ApprenticeshipsNI programme provides the opportunity to obtain vocational qualifications in a range of trade occupations including electrical, plumbing and construction.  Higher Level Apprenticeships offers qualifications at Level 4 and above in the areas of Construction, Planning and the Built Environment.  My Department’s annual Apprenticeship Week and biannual advertising campaign are also used to promote Apprenticeships, including trades.  Careers Advisers meet young people in schools in Year 12 to promote progression into all career areas, including trades. The development of a new Careers Portal will be a key action in the upcoming joint DfE/DE Careers action plan, ensuring everyone understands the pathways available to them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any additional assistance that her Department can provide to the PSNI to prevent the rise in vehicle ramming incidents against PSNI patrols.
answer_text: I find incidents such as this, against PSNI officers deplorable and a deliberate act of criminality, demonstrating little regard to others. This is why I intend to bring forward a new offence of assault on those providing services to the public, within a proposed Sentencing Bill which I hope will be introduced in this mandate. The new offence will have a higher maximum sentence in the magistrates’ courts than is currently available for the similar existing offences.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 10234/22-27, for an update on the crossing installation.
answer_text: As I previously advised in April 2024 a controlled crossing at Chapel Street, Poyntzpass has been placed on a list with other locations for which a pedestrian crossing has been deemed suitable. Unfortunately, due to constrained resources, this scheme was not included on the 2024/25 programme, but will be considered for inclusion in a future works programme, subject to available resources and prioritisation of other schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the findings of any condition report possessed by her Department on the current condition of Marlborough House, Craigavon.
answer_text: Marlborough House, Craigavon far exceeds the capacity needed for the civil service in the area and is the worst performing building in our Office Estate in terms of energy efficiency.My Department prepared a report in April 2021 on the building, which indicated that the mechanical plant and equipment was due for replacement and that the overall Heating, Ventilation and Air Conditioning (HVAC) strategy adopted in the building was no longer fit for purpose.This was followed up with an operational energy evaluation in April 2022 which assessed the HVAC system refurbishment options for the building. This report found that the building fabric is well out of date, with U-values (a measurement of how well a material or structure insulates heat) significantly greater than would currently be expected. It also reported that the HVAC plant is significantly past its usable life expectancy and not appropriate for a modern office. The report concluded that these elements of the building are essentially at end of life.To bring the building up to current standards for a modern office environment, including the installation of double glazed windows, insulating external walls, replacing the HVAC plant and equipment, and replacing fossil fuel powered plant with an electrically driven alternative, was costed in the region of £18.6m. This would not, however, represent the total cost of refurbishing Marlborough House which would also require redecoration, carpeting and furniture throughout, as well as providing interim accommodation for staff, decanting and recanting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the impact a listing by Historical Environment Division would have on the disposability and future use of Marlborough House, Craigavon.
answer_text: Marlborough House is significantly beyond the capacity needed by the Civil Service in the area and is among our worst performing buildings in terms of energy efficiency. My Department’s intention is to vacate and dispose of the site in early 2026. My Department has been in discussion with another public sector body with a potential interest in acquiring the site. They are currently working through a suitability and viability assessment of the property with their consultant team and Department for Communities (DfC) Historical Environment Division.My Department has raised concerns with DfC that, if the building were considered unsuitable, it may become derelict indefinitely once vacated by the civil service as the listing may impact negatively on any potential sale should it come onto the open market.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any discussions she has had with the Minister for Communities on the future use and issues associated with the potential listing of Marlborough House.
answer_text: Senior officials in my Department have made officials in the Department for Communities (DfC) aware that the Department intends to vacate the building and dispose of the site in early 2026. My Department has also made DfC aware that the listing of Marlborough House might have a detrimental impact on the future viability of the site for use by another public sector body. My Department also raised concerns that the building may become derelict indefinitely once the civil service vacates the site in December 2025 as the listing may impact negatively on any potential sale of the building should it come onto the open market.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any discussions he has had with his Department's Historic Environment Division in relation to local concerns around the proposed Listed Building status of Marlborough House, Craigavon.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting time for cataract surgery.
answer_text: In order to address waiting lists and times, a network of three Cataract Day Procedure Centres was established in 2018/19 to act as a resource for the region, working across Trust boundaries, to equalise and reduce regional waits, and to avoid clinic and theatre short-notice cancellations in more acute hospital settings due to competition with unscheduled care, beds, and anaesthetic cover.The Day Procedure Centre cataract service has had a positive impact on waiting times. At the 31 March 2024 there were approximately 3,000 patients waiting on regional surgical waiting lists for a cataract procedure, of whom 1,325 were waiting over 13 weeks. This is an improved position when compared to March 2022 when there was a total of 4,600 patients waiting for this procedure, of which, approximately 3,250 were waiting over 13 weeks. Current average waiting times on surgical waiting lists are laid out on Table 1 below:Average wait time (in weeks) for cataract operations1 in 2023/242HSC TrustMeanNumber of CasesBelfast24.81,151Northern22.61,606South Eastern181,376Southern17.41,668Western29.21,135HSC Trust Total21.96,936Source: Hospital Inpatient System, Hospital Activity Information Branch, Information & Analysis Directorate, Department of Health, NI.1Cataract procedures were identified using the following OPCS codes in the primary operation field: C711, C712, C713, C718, C719, C721, C722, C723, C728, C729, C731, C732, C733, C34, C738, C739, C741, C742, C743, C748, C749, or where any of these codes are in a secondary operation field, combined with the following in the primary operation field:C751, C752, C753, C754, C758, C759.2Only patients who were admitted from a waiting list, planned or booked have been included.3Date put on waiting list was not provided for 33 cases in Belfast Trust and 8 cases in Northern Trust, so these have been excluded from the data.4Due to ongoing validations in relation to date added to waiting list, cataract operations occurring in South Eastern HSCT, on or after 9th November 2023, have been excluded from the data.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 14865/22-27, why more up to date statistics are not available.
answer_text: The Department’s most recently published statistics on the uptake of Pension Credit were released on 01 September 2022 and provide estimates for the 2019/20 financial year.  The Department is not in a position to update these statistics at present due to data constraints relating to State Pension data. The Department is working closely with the Department for Work and Pensions to resolve these issues.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline how the Investment Strategy for Northern Ireland will improve transport links and roads in Newry and Armagh.
answer_text: The strategy identifies the priorities for investment at a high level.  It recognises the need for regional balance and the need for investment in improving transport links across the region.  The draft strategy includes investment in key road schemes and a major road maintenance programme, alongside investment in active travel and public transport.The detail of specific projects will be set out in departmental investment plans, and would be a matter for the relevant Ministers.Ensuring that all areas are supported to achieve economic growth and prosperity is a key priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his plan to address the significant amount of detritus built up along kerb edging at road junctions, traffic islands, traffic lights and bus stops in the Newry and Armagh constituency.
answer_text: The Litter (NI) Order states that responsibility for the removal of detritus from public roads rests with local councils with the exception of special roads specifically detailed in the Order. My Department has being working co-operatively with Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council on a pilot programme to clean footways and detritus along kerb lines at locations outside 40mph limits. This programme is aimed primarily at the restoration of overgrown and narrow rural footways to their original width and does not extend to the routine clearing of detritus from kerb lines. The work associated with the pilot has been delivered with costs shared equally between my Department and the Council. Several locations have recently been completed and further locations will be considered subject to the availability of funding and resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the grant aid support available for the installation of solar panels in domestic settings.
answer_text: There is currently no financial support available from my Department for homeowners who wish to install solar panels.As you may be aware, the Windsor Framework enables the British government to extend the VAT relief for Energy Saving Materials, previously available in Britian to the north. This means that people here will also be able to apply zero rates of VAT to the installation of energy-saving materials such as solar panels.Homeowners may also wish to seek independent advice on solar panels, and other energy efficiency matters from organisations such as the NI Energy Advice Service, the Energy Saving Trust and Citizen’s Advice. These technologies have the potential to provide an attractive payback to homeowners without grant support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of mechanical sweepers available for immediate use in the Armagh section area.
answer_text: Whilst my Department does not have any mechanical sweepers specifically assigned for use in the Armagh section area, a number are owned and maintained within the overall fleet. These can be used to the Armagh area to deal with large scale spillages.  My Department can also utilise external contractors to deal with immediate public safety issues.The sweeping of roads does not fall under the responsibility of my Department, rather this lies with local councils in accordance with the Litter (NI) Order.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the time frame for (i) the repair of road surface defects; and (ii) the replacement of worn white line markings, in Legacorry Park, Richhill, to enhance road safety for residents and those accessing the school.
answer_text: Having decided on indicative budget allocations (subject to a consultation on the equality impacts) my officials can now consider the detailed programmes of work across the Department. Once this work has concluded, the detailed work programmes will be presented at meetings with the relevant councils to be scheduled in due course. In the meantime, officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.The refurbishment of the road markings in Legacorry Park, Richhill has been added to my Department’s programme of road marking work. This work will be carried out when the resources are available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the financial assistance available to marching bands from his Department.
answer_text: The Musical Instruments Programme is designed and delivered by the Arts Council of Northern Ireland, an arms-length body of my Department, to improve the quality of music-making in the community by helping eligible bands, groups and artists to purchase new instruments and/or replace worn out instruments. The programme is open to all eligible applicants based in Northern Ireland and has 3 strands; bands, professional and non-professional groups and individuals, each with its own application process. The programme is competitive, and the number of awards is limited by the amount of funding available. This year’s Musical Instruments for Individuals scheme closed to applications on 2nd September 2024. The Musical Instruments for Bands & Musical Instruments for Professional & Non-professional Performing Groups scheme remain open for applications until 12noon on 30th September 2024. The overall budget across the 3 strands is £500k. The Arts Council’s Small Capital programme is another possible source of funding for, for example, band halls which meet its funding criteria.   The Arts Council also operates an Artists International Development Fund, however has recently closed to applications.  This programme is aimed at supporting early-stage networking and development of artistic practice through working and collaborating international/artistic residencies.  Although primarily aimed at individual artists, arts organisations can also apply to tour small scale work.  Bands are eligible to apply but grants are modest.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the new build process for Tandragee Junior High School.
answer_text: Tandragee Junior High School was announced under the major capital works programme in June 2020.A technical feasibility report, which detailed feasible options and costs for replacement accommodation, was completed by the appointed Integrated Consultant Team and submitted to the Department for review in August 2022.  Following submission, the owner of one of the sites advised that the site was no longer available. A revised technical feasibility report was submitted to the Department in June 2024 and is currently being reviewed by the Department’s technical advisors.  Following approval of the report a business case will be completed, and once approved the preferred option identified will be taken forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will commit to installing a 30mph speed limit to serve Milltown on the Aghory Road, Richhill.
answer_text: My Department’s policy for setting the appropriate speed limit for a road aims to ensure speed limits are consistent and motorists are presented with the same speed limit on similar roads taking into account factors such as: the characteristic of the road; the speed of existing traffic; the degree of frontage development; and road traffic collision data, which is continuously monitored using data provided by the PSNI. A review of injury collision data from the PSNI shows that there are no recorded injury collisions along this particular stretch of the Aghory Road within the last 3 recorded years.  The current speed limits on the Aghory Road, Richhill are in line with our policy document and considered to be appropriate.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding, or parking which causes an obstruction to other road (and footway) users should be directed to the police using the non-emergency number 101.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an assessment of the number of pensioners in Northern Ireland who are eligible for, but have not claimed, Pension Credit.
answer_text: This Department’s most recently published statistics on the uptake of Pension Credit were released on 01 September 2022 and provided estimates for the 2019/20 financial year.  The estimates indicate that 72% of eligible families (66,300) were in receipt of Pension Credit, while 28% (26,300) of families with potential entitlement to Pension Credit were not receiving it. My Department continues to work proactively to ensure that all eligible pensioners receive the benefits to which they are entitled.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the financial support that is available to pensioners this winter.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale behind the proposed closure of the phlebotomy drive-through blood clinic service at St Luke’s Hospital site, Armagh.
answer_text: Patients are often asked to go for a blood test with the phlebotomy service at a hospital or clinic before they attend an outpatient appointment. Phlebotomy is the procedure to take blood, usually from a vein in the arm, and is used to diagnose many medical conditions.Historically, phlebotomy clinics in the Southern HSC Trust area were offered from one of the Trust’s ‘blood rooms’ at Craigavon Area, Daisy Hill or South Tyrone hospitals before their clinical appointments. In response to the Covid-19 pandemic, to promote social distancing and reduce the risk of infection, temporary drive through phlebotomy clinics were funded in all Trusts in Northern Ireland. During this time, patients from across the Southern area, drove to the St Luke’s site in Armagh for their blood tests. Temporary funding for this service officially ceased on 31st March 2023. While other Trusts have already stopped providing drive through phlebotomy, the Southern Trust continued to run the service. This service has continued to operate at financial risk, at a cost of circa £230,000 per annum. In light of the Trust’s financial savings to be achieved in 2024/2025, cessation of this service was included in the Trust’s savings plan proposals. This proposal was approved by the Trust’s Senior Leadership Team and detailed within the Trust’s financial savings plans which were submitted to my Department for consideration.  Whilst my Department recognises that many patients, carers and staff greatly valued the convenience of the drive through option for blood tests from the dedicated and professional team at St Luke’s, given the current financial climate, my Department has accepted this proposal and the Trust is no longer in a financial position to continue to operate the drive through phlebotomy service from Friday, 28 June 2024.  From July, the Trust will return to pre-pandemic arrangements, with patients who require phlebotomy services continuing to be offered a service with the option to attend Craigavon, Daisy Hill or South Tyrone clinics.   Staff have been kept updated of the changes and will return to their substantive roles in blood clinics at Craigavon, Daisy Hill and South Tyrone hospitals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will commit to a review of the rating of empty homes policy.
answer_text: Given the acute housing shortage that the Executive is addressing it is still important to retain liability for empty homes to maximise use. I therefore have no plans to review the rating of empty homes.The circumstances of the policy’s introduction were similar to the situation we face now, in that there were issues with availability of much needed housing. The policy followed recommendations from the Semple Report into Housing Affordability and was carefully calibrated to prevent avoidance. My Department does however monitor specific issues that are raised in relation to the exclusions element of the policy and adjustments have been made since its introduction.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on fish restocking of the Public Angling Estate.
answer_text: The Department stocked out 76,000 juvenile Brown Trout fry in April 2024 to a small number of Public Angling Estate (PAE) waters. These fry had been reared on borehole water and were therefore not exposed to zebra mussels. Officials are continuing to work with the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute to develop a protocol that will prevent the transfer of zebra mussels and allow stocking of fish to the PAE.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what resources he will allocate to grass verge cutting to improve visibility when exiting junctions.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline all support that is available to people starting to study to become a nurse.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to speed limits for goods vehicles exceeding 7.5 tonnes, to detail (i) why these vehicles are restricted to 40mph on single carriageways; and (ii) his plans to increase this limit to 50mph in order to bring Northern Ireland on par with England and Wales.
answer_text: Speed limits for Heavy Goods Vehicles (HGVs) exceeding 7.5 tonnes are lower than other vehicles as they are heavier and therefore have longer stopping distance.The Department is aware that the speed limits for HGVs over 7.5 tonnes was changed in England and Wales, although I’m advised that this has not been replicated in Scotland.  While I can confirm that at present there are no plans to change HGV speed limits here, our approach to speed limits in general is one of the topics that I intend to discuss with officials during my time in office.     One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the steps being taken to ensure the Investment Strategy for Northern Ireland creates economic opportunity and regeneration in Newry and Armagh.
answer_text: The draft ISNI, Infrastructure 2050, sets out a strategy for investment in infrastructure for the next 30 years to stabilise, deliver and transform.  It recognises the need for regional balance across the investment priorities.It aims to ensure that projects help create thriving communities and places, a clean, secure, and sustainable environment, and a competitive and connected economy, and it seeks to build resilience in public services.The long-term vision of the ISNI will be supported by a medium-term Investment Plan.  This demonstrates how we will make best use of our available financial resources over the next ten years across key infrastructure sectors.  The Investment Plan will detail initial priority projects across the region.  The prioritisation framework developed for the draft ISNI includes a specific criterion addressing regional balance.  As the strategy remains in draft, it is not possible to go into specifics on individual projects but ensuring that all areas are supported to achieve economic growth and prosperity is a key priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of farm-related planning applications responded to positively by the NIEA using the ammonia interim assessment approach, following the decision to cease relying on published ammonia advice as a basis for statutory advice on planning applications.
answer_text: In DAERA’s role as a statutory consultee the Department retains information on planning consultation information associated with a range of development types. Farm-related planning applications is not a classification identifiable through existing Departmental IT databases.I can however advise that since the 19 December 2023 until 31 May 2024 the NIEA has responded to 95 agricultural related cases, 48 of these cases have been assessed under the ammonia interim assessment approach. The NIEA has no air quality concerns with 6 of these cases, has air quality concerns in 7 cases, with further information being requested for the other cases. Whilst acknowledging the specific advice provided above regarding air quality it is recognised that these consultations may also be subject to further advice from DAERA about other environmental impacts. Consequently on the basis of the information available, DAERA is content with the development as proposed in 4 cases.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any discussions she has had with the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs on a financial assistance scheme for farmers affected by flooding.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 23445/17-22, to detail (i) the outcome of safety considerations for class B+E trailer test license changes; and (ii) when the changes will be enacted.
answer_text: Legislation introduced in Britain on 16 December 2021 removed testing requirements for towing a trailer (BE entitlement), meaning that all drivers who passed a category B (car) driving test from 1 January 1997 are now entitled to tow trailers up to 3,500kg Maximum Authorized Mass (MAM).  This change was made in an effort to address the HGV driver shortage situation in England, Scotland and Wales.At that time testing was not seen as a significant reason for HGV driver shortages here. This position has not changed.In EU Member States BE testing is still a requirement, as it is here. Road safety continues to be of paramount importance and therefore I have no plans to introduce similar changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on progress of the pedestrian crossing request for Poyntzpass.
answer_text: My Department’s officials have assessed Chapel Street (A27), Poyntzpass for a controlled pedestrian crossing using the criteria set out in the Department’s current policy document: RSPPG E005 (Controlled Crossings: Assessment Procedures), which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/controlled-crossings-assessment-procedures-rsppg-e005The survey confirmed the area met the Department’s criteria for the installation of a controlled crossing and it has been placed on a list with other locations for which a pedestrian crossing has been assessed as suitable.  Once the Department’s budget is confirmed for the new financial year, this proposal will be considered for inclusion within our 2024/25 work programme subject to funding availability.
